topics.title: Java Memory Model & Concurrency Primitives
topics.description: The Java Memory Model (JMM) defines how threads interact through memory — when writes by one thread become visible to others. Without the JMM guarantees, compilers and CPUs are free to reorder instructions for optimization, causing subtle concurrency bugs. Key primitives: `volatile` (visibility without atomicity), `synchronized` (mutual exclusion + visibility), `Atomic*` classes (lock-free atomicity via CAS), and the happens-before relationship.
topics.story: Two programmers (threads) work on separate whiteboards (CPU caches). Each has a scratchpad copy of the shared code. Without any rules, programmer A's update might stay on their scratchpad for minutes before the other sees it. `volatile` is like a shared Google Doc — changes are immediately visible to all. `synchronized` is like a meeting room with a key — only one person inside at a time, and when they leave, everyone sees all their changes. `AtomicInteger` is like an increment machine with a lock — you can press the button, it atomically reads, increments, and updates.
topics.analogy: happens-before: if A happens-before B, B sees all of A's writes. volatile write happens-before any subsequent volatile read. synchronized exit happens-before the next synchronized entry on the same lock. Thread.start() happens-before any action in the started thread. Visibility bug: thread caches stale value in CPU register. Atomicity bug: check-then-act race (read 0, both write 1 simultaneously). volatile: visibility yes, atomicity no (i++ is 3 ops). AtomicInteger: visibility yes, atomicity yes via CAS.
topics.memoryAnchor: volatile: guarantees visibility, not atomicity. volatile int x; — safe for flags, NOT for increment. synchronized: mutual exclusion + memory barrier (flushes cache). AtomicInteger.incrementAndGet() — atomic compare-and-swap. AtomicReference<T> — atomic object reference swap. happens-before: monitor unlock → subsequent lock. volatile write → subsequent volatile read. Thread.start() → first action of started thread. ThreadLocal<T> — per-thread value, no sharing. LongAdder — high-contention counter (better than AtomicLong under contention).
topics.firstPrinciples: Modern CPUs have L1/L2/L3 caches. Each core has its own L1. Without synchronization, a thread may read stale values from its cache. The JMM establishes 'happens-before' ordering: if action A happens-before B, then B is guaranteed to see all effects of A. Compilers and CPUs can reorder instructions unless happens-before prevents it. volatile: inserting a memory barrier — flushes the store buffer, invalidates load buffer — ensures the latest value is always read/written to main memory. synchronized: acquires a monitor lock, which implies a happens-before from the previous release on the same lock. CAS (Compare-And-Swap): CPU instruction that atomically reads, compares to expected, and writes if equal — foundation of all java.util.concurrent lock-free algorithms.
topics.bruteForce: Counter counter = new Counter(); // shared. In multiple threads: counter.value++ — race condition. Read-modify-write is 3 non-atomic operations. Two threads can read the same value, both increment, and both write the same result — losing increments.
topics.optimizedApproach: AtomicInteger for single-variable counters. ReentrantLock or synchronized for compound actions spanning multiple variables. ConcurrentHashMap for concurrent map access. volatile for boolean flags (running = false to stop a loop). LongAdder for high-contention increment-only counters.
topics.whenToUse: volatile: stop-flag boolean in producer/consumer, double-checked locking with volatile (Java 5+). AtomicInteger/Long: thread-safe counters, sequence generators. AtomicReference: lock-free stack/queue head pointer. synchronized: compound check-then-act that must be atomic. ThreadLocal: per-request context (DB transaction, request ID, user session) in web servers.

examples.title: Volatile Flag + Atomic Counter + Synchronized
examples.explanation: volatile ensures the worker thread sees the updated `running` flag immediately — without it the JVM might cache the value in a register forever. AtomicInteger uses CAS (compare-and-swap) to increment atomically — 100 threads × 1000 increments always gives exactly 100,000. synchronized adds a happens-before memory barrier ensuring all threads see each other's updates. ThreadLocal gives each thread its own isolated copy — no sharing, no synchronization needed.
examples.realWorldUse: Spring's RequestContextHolder uses ThreadLocal to store the current HttpServletRequest per thread. Servlet containers give each request its own thread; the ThreadLocal is set at the filter level and cleaned up after the request. Web MVC's @Transactional stores the current database connection in a TransactionSynchronizationManager backed by a ThreadLocal.

problems.title: Thread-Safe Counter with AtomicInteger
problems.description:
Start 50 threads, each incrementing a shared counter 200 times. Print the final count.

Use `AtomicInteger` to make the counter thread-safe.

Expected output: `10000`
problems.hint1: Create an AtomicInteger counter = new AtomicInteger(0).
problems.hint2: Each thread calls counter.incrementAndGet() in a loop 200 times.
problems.hint3: Use ExecutorService with awaitTermination to wait for all threads, then print counter.get().
problems.editorial: AtomicInteger.incrementAndGet() is a single atomic operation implemented via CPU CAS. Unlike `count++` which is read-modify-write (3 operations, can interleave), CAS atomically updates the value only if it still matches the expected value, retrying on contention. The result is always exactly 50 × 200 = 10,000.

problems.title: Compare-and-Swap with AtomicReference
problems.description:
Implement a thread-safe stack using `AtomicReference` (lock-free, using CAS).

The stack holds Integers. Operations:
- `push(int val)` — adds to top
- `pop()` — returns and removes top, or -1 if empty

Read N operations from stdin:
- `PUSH val` — push the value
- `POP` — print the popped value

Sample input:
```
PUSH 10
PUSH 20
POP
PUSH 30
POP
POP
```
Expected:
```
20
30
10
```
problems.hint1: Define a Node<T> with value and next. AtomicReference<Node<Integer>> head = new AtomicReference<>(null).
problems.hint2: For push: loop with compareAndSet(current, new Node(val, current)) until CAS succeeds.
problems.hint3: For pop: loop with compareAndSet(current, current.next) until CAS succeeds or stack is empty.
problems.sampleInput:
PUSH 10
PUSH 20
POP
PUSH 30
POP
POP
problems.editorial: Lock-free stack using AtomicReference and CAS. compareAndSet(expected, update) atomically checks if head still equals expected — if yes, updates to new value and returns true. If another thread changed head between our read and CAS, it returns false and we retry the loop. This avoids locks while being thread-safe. The ABA problem (value changed A→B→A) exists here but is acceptable for this stack.

problems.title: Volatile Stop Flag
problems.description:
A producer thread generates numbers 1, 2, 3... continuously and adds them to a shared `LinkedBlockingQueue<Integer>`. A consumer thread reads from the queue and prints each number. After 100ms, use a `volatile boolean stopped` flag to stop both threads.

Print the count of numbers produced (and consumed) — they must be equal. Print: `Produced: N` and `Consumed: N` where N >= 1.

Note: exact N will vary; just verify Produced == Consumed by checking both lines have the same number.
problems.hint1: Declare `static volatile boolean stopped = false;`. Producer loops while !stopped.
problems.hint3: Use AtomicInteger for produced/consumed counts. After setting stopped=true, join both threads before printing.
problems.editorial: volatile boolean stopped ensures the producer thread sees the flag change immediately without caching it in a register. Without volatile, the JVM can hoist the stopped check out of the loop (infinite loop optimization). LinkedBlockingQueue is thread-safe. The consumer checks !stopped || !queue.isEmpty() to drain remaining items after the producer stops.
